growth_records.medium: Nutrient-rich mineral medium (Van de Waal et al. 2009), CO2/carbon-limited

up.method: reported max uptake rate (umax,CO2), model-fitted from monoculture (Table 2); half-saturation HCO2=2.0 umol/L
reported max uptake rate (umax,HCO3), model-fitted from monoculture (Table 2); half-saturation HHCO3=200 umol/L

sec.method: reported max respiration rate (rmax), model-fitted from monoculture (Table 2)

growth_records.cite: Van de Waal et al. Reversal in competitive dominance of a toxic versus non-toxic cyanobacterium in response to rising CO2. The ISME Journal (2011). DOI:10.1038/ismej.2011.28. [PMC3160686]
growth_records.snippet: The chemostats were run at a dilution rate of D=0.011 h-1... Table 2: umax 0.80 per day; umax,CO2 11.2, umax,HCO3 9.5 umol mm-3 per day; rmax 1.0 umol mm-3 per day (CYA43).
growth_records.notes: Prokaryote (cyanobacterium) chemostat monoculture, CO2/carbon-limited. Growth: reported maximum specific growth rate umax=0.80 per day (Table 2) -> per h = 0.80/24 = 0.0333 h-1; doubling_time = ln2/umax = ln2/0.80 day = 0.866 day = 20.8 h. Chemostat operated below umax at D=0.011 h-1; at steady state net specific growth = D = 0.011 h-1 (specific loss rate m=0.264 per day also reported). Uptake and respiration rates are biovolume-specific (umol per mm3 biovolume per day), model-fitted maxima from Table 2, NOT converted to per-gDW (no biomass DW-to-biovolume conversion given). Steady-state chemostat measured dissolved CO2 ~0.4 umol/L and pH ~9.8 for this non-toxic strain (Results); influx medium pH ~8.2. Cell volume 47-87e-9 mm3/cell. Full mineral medium composition referenced to Van de Waal et al. 2009, not given in-paper; only carbon-system components stated here.

growth_records.medium: Nutrient-rich mineral medium (Van de Waal et al. 2009), CO2/carbon-limited

up.method: reported max uptake rate (umax,CO2), model-fitted from monoculture (Table 2); half-saturation HCO2=0.5 umol/L
reported max uptake rate (umax,HCO3), model-fitted from monoculture (Table 2); half-saturation HHCO3=75 umol/L

sec.method: reported max respiration rate (rmax), model-fitted from monoculture (Table 2)

growth_records.cite: Van de Waal et al. Reversal in competitive dominance of a toxic versus non-toxic cyanobacterium in response to rising CO2. The ISME Journal (2011). DOI:10.1038/ismej.2011.28. [PMC3160686]
growth_records.snippet: Table 2: umax 0.86 per day; umax,CO2 8.2, umax,HCO3 7.3 umol mm-3 per day; rmax 1.1 umol mm-3 per day; MC 0.52 ug mm-3 (CYA140). D=0.011 h-1.
growth_records.notes: Prokaryote (cyanobacterium) chemostat monoculture, CO2/carbon-limited, toxic microcystin-LR producing strain. Growth: reported maximum specific growth rate umax=0.86 per day (Table 2) -> per h = 0.86/24 = 0.0358 h-1; doubling_time = ln2/umax = ln2/0.86 day = 0.806 day = 19.3 h. Chemostat operated below umax at D=0.011 h-1; at steady state net specific growth = D = 0.011 h-1 (specific loss rate m=0.264 per day reported). Uptake/respiration rates biovolume-specific (umol per mm3 biovolume per day), model-fitted maxima from Table 2, not converted to per-gDW (no DW/biovolume factor given). Steady-state chemostat measured dissolved CO2 ~0.2 umol/L and pH ~10 for this toxic strain (Results); influx medium pH ~8.2. Cellular microcystin content 0.52 ug/mm3 (extracellular microcystin <2% of total, negligible secretion). Cell volume 35e-9 mm3/cell. Full mineral medium composition referenced to Van de Waal et al. 2009, not given in-paper.

growth_records.medium: BG-11 with 2 mM sodium nitrate (high N)
growth_records.cite: Wei Li et al. Microbiome processing of organic nitrogen input supports growth and cyanotoxin production of Microcystis aeruginosa cultures. The ISME Journal (2024). DOI:10.1093/ismejo/wrae082. [PMC11126159]
growth_records.snippet: The PCC7806 (originally axenic) cultures ... exhibited detectable increases in chlorophyll autofluorescence under all tested N sources, fastest with 2 mM nitrate (0.16 d-1) and slowest with protein (0.06 d-1; Fig. 4)
growth_records.notes: Specific growth rate reported as 0.16 d-1 (measured from in vivo chlorophyll-a fluorescence slope, plate reader Ex440/Em680). Converted to per-h: 0.16/24 = 0.00667 h-1. Doubling time = ln2/mu = 0.693/0.16 = 4.33 d = 103.9 h. Base medium BG-11 (full composition not enumerated in text); only N source (2 mM NaNO3) specified. Cyanobacterium = prokaryote. No mmol/gDW/h uptake/secretion rates in text; N incorporation tracked by nanoSIMS but only shown in figures.

growth_records.medium: BG-11 with protein as sole N source (50 uM N)
growth_records.cite: Wei Li et al. Microbiome processing of organic nitrogen input supports growth and cyanotoxin production of Microcystis aeruginosa cultures. The ISME Journal (2024). DOI:10.1093/ismejo/wrae082. [PMC11126159]
growth_records.snippet: fastest with 2 mM nitrate (0.16 d-1) and slowest with protein (0.06 d-1; Fig. 4), showing this strain could directly utilize organic N in both simple (AA) and complex (protein) form
growth_records.notes: Reported specific growth rate 0.06 d-1 on protein as sole N source (axenic PCC7806). Converted: 0.06/24 = 0.0025 h-1. Doubling time = 0.693/0.06 = 11.55 d = 277.3 h. Protein supplied at 50 uM N. Full BG-11 composition not enumerated.

growth_records.medium: BG-11 with protein as sole N source (50 uM N)
growth_records.cite: Wei Li et al. Microbiome processing of organic nitrogen input supports growth and cyanotoxin production of Microcystis aeruginosa cultures. The ISME Journal (2024). DOI:10.1093/ismejo/wrae082. [PMC11126159]
growth_records.snippet: with protein as the sole N source, the microbiome from LE19 (bacterized-), but not from LE3 (bacterized+), led to an increase in growth rates (from 0.07 to 0.10 d-1; Tukey HSD, P < 0.01)
growth_records.notes: Growth rate on protein rose from 0.07 d-1 (with LE3 microbiome / bacterized+) to 0.10 d-1 with LE19 microbiome. This record = LE19 microbiome, 0.10 d-1. Converted: 0.10/24 = 0.004167 h-1; doubling = 0.693/0.10 = 6.93 d = 166.3 h. Demonstrates microbiome-mediated ammonification/organic-N modification boosting cyanobacterial N supply.

growth_records.medium: BG-11 with protein as sole N source (50 uM N)
growth_records.cite: Wei Li et al. Microbiome processing of organic nitrogen input supports growth and cyanotoxin production of Microcystis aeruginosa cultures. The ISME Journal (2024). DOI:10.1093/ismejo/wrae082. [PMC11126159]
growth_records.snippet: led to an increase in growth rates (from 0.07 to 0.10 d-1; Tukey HSD, P < 0.01)
growth_records.notes: Baseline protein growth rate 0.07 d-1 (with LE3 microbiome / bacterized+, no significant boost). Converted: 0.07/24 = 0.002917 h-1; doubling = 0.693/0.07 = 9.9 d = 237.7 h.

growth_records.medium: Modified CT medium (Watanabe and Ichimura 1977), N-limited (81 uM N)

up.method: N-limiting substrate (ion chromatography of residual NO3-); nitrate is the growth-limiting nutrient but a biomass-specific uptake rate is not reported

sec.units: ug per mm3 cell biovolume per day
sec.method: HPLC/LC quantification of particulate microcystin normalized to culture biovolume; baseline 26C mean across N conditions (range 0.08-0.14)

growth_records.cite: Martin et al. Episodic Decrease in Temperature Increases mcy Gene Transcription and Cellular Microcystin in Continuous Cultures of Microcystis aeruginosa PCC 7806. Frontiers in Microbiology (2020). DOI:10.3389/fmicb.2020.601864. [PMC7744600]
growth_records.snippet: Dilution rate (D) was maintained at 0.25 d-1 for all experiments reported here. ... Steady-state (baseline) cell concentration at 26C was ~2.3-, 5.3-, and 8.6 x 10^6 cells mL-1 in 81 uM, 161 uM, and 323 uM N, respectively.
growth_records.notes: Chemostat at fixed D=0.25 d-1. At steady state mu=D=0.25 d-1; converted to per hour: 0.25/24=0.01042 h-1. Doubling time td=ln2/mu=0.693/0.25=2.77 d=66.5 h. Steady-state (baseline) at 26C for the 81 uM N vessel; steady-state cell density ~2.3e6 cells mL-1. Growth rate is CALCULATED from the chemostat identity mu=D (paper states cultures were at steady state at 26C; at 19C growth fell below D and cells washed out, so 19C is not a steady state and no valid mu). Nitrate is the growth-limiting substrate (residual NO3- measured by ion chromatography, Dionex ICS2100) but no biomass-specific uptake rate (mmol/gDW/h) is reported; biomass reported as cells mL-1 / biovolume, not gDW, so qS not derivable. Microcystin production 0.11 ug mm-3 d-1 (baseline mean across N levels, range 0.08-0.14) is intracellular/particulate content normalized to biovolume, not a true extracellular secretion flux. pH 8.2, T=26C, continuous light 50 umol photons m-2 s-1.

growth_records.medium: Modified CT medium (Watanabe and Ichimura 1977), N-limited (161 uM N)

up.method: N-limiting substrate; biomass-specific uptake rate not reported

sec.units: ug per mm3 cell biovolume per day

growth_records.cite: Martin et al. Episodic Decrease in Temperature Increases mcy Gene Transcription and Cellular Microcystin in Continuous Cultures of Microcystis aeruginosa PCC 7806. Frontiers in Microbiology (2020). DOI:10.3389/fmicb.2020.601864. [PMC7744600]
growth_records.snippet: Steady-state (baseline) cell concentration at 26C was ~2.3-, 5.3-, and 8.6 x 10^6 cells mL-1 in 81 uM, 161 uM, and 323 uM N, respectively.
growth_records.notes: Same chemostat D=0.25 d-1 -> mu=D=0.25 d-1=0.01042 h-1; td=ln2/0.25=2.77 d=66.5 h. 161 uM N vessel, steady-state cell density ~5.3e6 cells mL-1 at 26C. Nitrate is growth-limiting; no qS in mmol/gDW/h reported. Microcystin value is the cross-condition baseline biovolume-normalized intracellular content.

growth_records.medium: Modified CT medium (Watanabe and Ichimura 1977), N-replete relative (323 uM N)

up.method: substrate measured by ion chromatography; biomass-specific uptake rate not reported

sec.units: ug per mm3 cell biovolume per day

growth_records.cite: Martin et al. Episodic Decrease in Temperature Increases mcy Gene Transcription and Cellular Microcystin in Continuous Cultures of Microcystis aeruginosa PCC 7806. Frontiers in Microbiology (2020). DOI:10.3389/fmicb.2020.601864. [PMC7744600]
growth_records.snippet: Dilution rate (D) was maintained at 0.25 d-1 for all experiments reported here. ... 8.6 x 10^6 cells mL-1 in ... 323 uM N.
growth_records.notes: Chemostat D=0.25 d-1 -> mu=D=0.25 d-1=0.01042 h-1; td=ln2/0.25=2.77 d=66.5 h. 323 uM N vessel, steady-state cell density ~8.6e6 cells mL-1 at 26C. Nitrate not fully consumed at highest N:P; no qS (mmol/gDW/h). Microcystin value is cross-condition baseline biovolume-normalized intracellular content.

growth_records.medium: modified WC medium
growth_records.cite: Christian Burberg et al. Phosphate Limitation Increases Content of Protease Inhibitors in the Cyanobacterium Microcystis aeruginosa. Toxins (Basel) (2020). DOI:10.3390/toxins12010033. [PMC7020438]
growth_records.snippet: Modeled growth parameters of M. aeruginosa grown with 8 different initial PO4 3- concentrations. The model was based on POC ... 5 [uM] ... K 93.1 +/- 3.6 ug C/mL, mumax 0.24 +/- 0.03 d-1
growth_records.notes: Phototrophic prokaryote; no substrate uptake or product secretion flux reported (CO2/light autotroph, P-limited batch). growth_rate_per_h converted from POC-based mumax=0.24 d-1 (Table 2): 0.24/24=0.0100 h-1; doubling_time=ln2/0.0100=69.3 h. Cell-abundance-based mumax for this treatment (Table 1)=0.34 d-1 (=0.0142 h-1). mumax is a logistic-fit maximal growth rate, so growth_method=calculated. Medium 'modified WC' cited from ref [75], composition not stated in text. pH not reported.

growth_records.medium: modified WC medium
growth_records.cite: Christian Burberg et al. Phosphate Limitation Increases Content of Protease Inhibitors in the Cyanobacterium Microcystis aeruginosa. Toxins (Basel) (2020). DOI:10.3390/toxins12010033. [PMC7020438]
growth_records.snippet: The model was based on POC ... 10 [uM] ... K 106.2 +/- 4.6 ug C/mL, mumax 0.32 +/- 0.004 d-1
growth_records.notes: P-limited photoautotrophic batch, no measured uptake/secretion flux. growth_rate_per_h from POC-based mumax=0.32 d-1 (Table 2): 0.32/24=0.01333 h-1; doubling_time=ln2/0.01333=52.0 h. Cell-based mumax (Table 1)=0.33 d-1. pH/medium composition not stated.

growth_records.cite: Christian Burberg et al. Phosphate Limitation Increases Content of Protease Inhibitors in the Cyanobacterium Microcystis aeruginosa. Toxins (Basel) (2020). DOI:10.3390/toxins12010033. [PMC7020438]
growth_records.snippet: The model was based on POC ... 15 [uM] ... K 272.9 +/- 9.2 ug C/mL, mumax 0.41 +/- 0.04 d-1
growth_records.notes: P-limited photoautotrophic batch, no measured uptake/secretion flux. growth_rate_per_h from POC-based mumax=0.41 d-1 (Table 2): 0.41/24=0.01708 h-1; doubling_time=ln2/0.01708=40.6 h. Cell-based mumax (Table 1)=0.45 d-1. pH/medium composition not stated.

growth_records.cite: Christian Burberg et al. Phosphate Limitation Increases Content of Protease Inhibitors in the Cyanobacterium Microcystis aeruginosa. Toxins (Basel) (2020). DOI:10.3390/toxins12010033. [PMC7020438]
growth_records.snippet: The model was based on POC ... 75 [uM] ... K 473.4 +/- 9.6 ug C/mL, mumax 0.45 +/- 0.04 d-1

Cell-based mumax (Table 1)=0.33 d-1. pH/medium composition not stated. This is the highest-P (least P-limited) treatment.

growth_records.medium: BG-11 (modified, 10 mM NaNO3)
growth_records.cite: Tim Piel et al. Suppressing Cyanobacteria with Hydrogen Peroxide Is More Effective at High Light Intensities. Toxins (2020). DOI:10.3390/toxins12010018. [PMC7020451]
growth_records.snippet: Microcystis was cultured in the nutrient-rich BG-11 medium [106] with 10 mmol/L NaNO3. The dilution rate of the chemostats was set at 0.01 h-1... The temperature in the chemostats was maintained at 25 C... aerated with pressurized air conta
growth_records.notes: Photoautotrophic cyanobacterium in light-limited steady-state chemostat, D=0.01 h-1 = mu at steady state; td=ln2/0.01=69.3 h. Incident irradiance 15 umol photons/m2/s. No substrate uptake or product secretion rates were quantified (photoautotroph; CO2 fixation and O2 evolution not measured as rates). Full BG-11 composition given only by citation [106]; only the modified NaNO3 concentration (10 mM) is stated in text, so medium_composition_stated=false. Downstream H2O2 dose-response assays were batch stress/toxicity tests (not growth conditions) and are not recorded. A minor heterotrophic bacterial contamination (~5% of cell counts) was noted at experiment end.

growth_records.medium: 5% N-free BG-11 + vitamin B12, low-N treatment

up.method: (322-3)ug/L over 5 days (drops to <3 ug/L by day 5)

growth_records.cite: Wang et al. Dynamic Phycobilin Pigment Variations in Diazotrophic and Non-diazotrophic Cyanobacteria Batch Cultures Under Different Initial Nitrogen Concentrations. Frontiers in Microbiology (2022). DOI:10.3389/fmicb.2022.850997. [PMC9201475]
growth_records.snippet: Microcystis aeruginosa strain 2385... 322 ... ug/L as nitrate N for low-N... The M. aeruginosa populations reached maximum biomass of 1.3 x 10^9 cells/L in 322 ug/L initial N... Nitrate-N concentration dropped from 322 ug/L to less than 3 u
growth_records.notes: Cyanobacterium (prokaryote), photoautotrophic batch. Growth rate is average NET rate over the batch, NOT maximal exponential mu (paper defines mu=ln(C2/C1)/(t2-t1) but tabulates no single value). mu=ln(1.3e9/9.0e8)/13 day=ln(1.444)/13=0.0283/day=0.00118/h; td=ln2/0.0283=24.5 day=588 h (13 d = midpoint of stated 12-14 d to max). Nutrient uptake given as VOLUMETRIC drawdown from measured concentrations (not per-gDW; biomass only as cells/L). NO3-N: (322-3)/5=63.8 ug N/L/day; SRP: (357-60)/14=21.2 ug P/L/day. Temp 26C, light 100 umol/m2/s. pH not reported.

growth_records.medium: 5% N-free BG-11 + vitamin B12, intermediate-N treatment
growth_records.cite: Wang et al. Dynamic Phycobilin Pigment Variations in Diazotrophic and Non-diazotrophic Cyanobacteria Batch Cultures Under Different Initial Nitrogen Concentrations. Frontiers in Microbiology (2022). DOI:10.3389/fmicb.2022.850997. [PMC9201475]
growth_records.snippet: 4.3 x 10^9 cells/L in 2,576 ug/L initial N... nitrate-N concentration was less than 3 ug/L on Day 9 for M. aeruginosa... SRP concentrations decreased to ~3 ug/L on Day 9 for M. aeruginosa cultures.
growth_records.notes: Average net growth rate over batch: mu=ln(4.3e9/9.0e8)/13=ln(4.778)/13=0.1203/day=0.00501/h; td=ln2/0.1203=5.76 day=138 h. NO3-N uptake (2576-3)/9=285.9 ug N/L/day; SRP (357-3)/9=39.3 ug P/L/day. Volumetric (not per-gDW). See low-N record for method caveats.

growth_records.medium: 5% N-free BG-11 + vitamin B12, high-N treatment
growth_records.cite: Wang et al. Dynamic Phycobilin Pigment Variations in Diazotrophic and Non-diazotrophic Cyanobacteria Batch Cultures Under Different Initial Nitrogen Concentrations. Frontiers in Microbiology (2022). DOI:10.3389/fmicb.2022.850997. [PMC9201475]
growth_records.snippet: 1.4 x 10^10 cells/L in 16,128 ug/L initial N... Nitrate-N concentration only fell to ~6,000 ug/L for M. aeruginosa... the SRP pool was exhausted on Day 7 for M. aeruginosa cultures.
growth_records.notes: mu=ln(1.4e10/9.0e8)/13=ln(15.56)/13=0.2111/day=0.0088/h; td=ln2/0.2111=3.28 day=78.8 h. Nitrate not exhausted (fell to ~6000 ug/L); P-limited (SRP exhausted day 7). NO3-N uptake (16128-6000)/14=723.4 ug N/L/day; SRP 357/7=51.0 ug P/L/day. Volumetric only.
